Why would we stop here?” He said humans are “instantly adaptable,” and that AGI will unlock “amazing new things” rather than halt human creativity.Along with this, the Google DeepMind CEO also provided a timeline that sits among a wide range of predictions from other AI leaders:* Sam Altman has said humanity is close to building digital superintelligence.* Dario Amodei predicted powerful AI could emerge as early as late 2026.* Jack Clark and Marina Favaro wrote that by 2027, AI systems could complete tasks that take humans weeks.* Yann LeCun has dismissed the concept of general intelligence as “complete BS,” arguing current transformer models cannot reach human‑level cognition.* DeepMind’s chief AGI scientist Shane Legg estimates a 50% chance of “minimal AGI” by 2028.Recently, Google DeepMind CEO argued it's time someone started policing it—and that the United States should hold the badge. In a manifesto titled "A Framework for Frontier AI and the Dawning of a New Age," the Nobel laureate laid out his most concrete regulatory pitch yet: a new standards body empowered to test the world's most advanced models before they ship, and, if the risk demanded it, to coordinate an industry-wide slowdown.Hassabis is specific about the shape of the thing. A conventional government agency, he argues, "would not be able to move fast enough, or have the right resources." So instead he wants a public-private body modelled on the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority—the self-regulator that oversees US brokers and markets. Fund it largely through industry, staff it with independent technical experts and open-source representatives, and keep it answerable to the federal government.The mechanics are elegant, which Hassabis knows. A model gets tagged "frontier-class" once it clears certain benchmark thresholds, and its maker becomes a "frontier lab" carrying extra responsibilities. That neatly sidesteps the endless fight over whether academic or open-source systems should be regulated—capability is the only thing that counts, not who built it or whether the code is public. Labs would share models with the body up to 30 days before release, voluntarily at first, before the review calcifies into a hard requirement for the US market.
Article Summary: Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis says AGI could arrive around 2030. He says society must prepare for the profound implications of AGI. Hassabis suggests US- lead watchdog for AI companies across the world. He also said humans are “instantly adaptable,” and that AGI will unlock “amazing new things”

Article Title: Oracle founder Larry Ellison wanted to buy Apple and fire its CEO, but Steve Jobs stopped him said: I do not need any…
Article Text:
Larry Ellison had $5 billion lined up to buy Apple and make Steve Jobs CEO—but Jobs said no

Larry Ellison had the $5 billion Apple takeover cash lined up

Why Steve Jobs chose the moral high ground over a hostile takeover

How Steve Jobs won control of Apple without owning it

Larry Ellison once had the money, the credit, and a fully worked-out plan to buy Apple outright. What he did not have was Steve Jobs' agreement. The Oracle co-founder revealed in a 2016 USC commencement speech that back in the mid-1990s, when Apple was worth roughly Rs 41,500 crore—about $5 billion—he had arranged to borrow every dollar needed to seize control of the struggling company and reinstall his close friend as CEO.The catch was Jobs himself. Ellison's pitch was blunt: buy Apple, hand Jobs the top job, done. But Jobs turned it down flat, telling Ellison he did not want power handed to him through a takeover. He wanted Apple to ask him back on its own terms—and he was not interested in the payday either."My idea was simple, buy Apple, and immediately make Steve CEO," Ellison said, recalling the plan. He pegged the price at around $5 billion and noted both men had strong credit. "I had already arranged to borrow all of the money. All Steve had to do was say yes." At the time Apple was hemorrhaging cash, shedding staff, and watching its stock sink to decade lows under CEO Gil Amelio. Wall Street had all but written it off.Jobs, then running NeXT after his 1985 ouster from Apple, saw a cleaner route. Rather than storm the company, he wanted Apple to acquire NeXT, ease him onto the board, and let the directors arrive at the obvious conclusion themselves.When Ellison pushed back with the operator's question—if they did not buy Apple, how would they make money—Jobs' reply became the line everyone remembers. "You don't need any more money," he told Ellison. "I'm not doing this for the money. I don't want to get paid." According to Walter Isaacson's biography, Jobs added that returning without either of them owning a stake would give him the moral high ground with employees and the industry.Ellison's retort was pure Ellison. He called it "the most expensive real estate on earth." A takeover, Jobs believed, would leave him legitimacy-poor in a company he needed to rally, and legitimacy mattered more to him than a controlling share.Jobs got his way. Apple bought NeXT in December 1996 for $429 million, brought Jobs in as an advisor, and by September 1997 he was interim CEO. The turnaround that followed—the iMac, the Microsoft investment truce, and eventually the iPhone—rebuilt Apple into the most valuable company on the planet, now worth well over $3.7 trillion.Ellison never got his takeover, but he got the outcome he actually wanted: Jobs back at the helm. He would join Apple's board that year and stay until 2002, stepping down when his schedule no longer allowed the commitment.
Article Summary: Larry Ellison had $5 billion lined up to buy Apple and make Steve Jobs CEO. Jobs turned it down flat, telling Ellison he did not want power handed to him through a takeover. Ellison never got his takeover, but he got the outcome he actually wanted: Jobs back at the helm.

Article Title: A memory shortage should scare a brand Lava's size, but MD Sunil Raina says it's an opening
Article Text:
Sunil Raina, Managing Director at Lava

A global memory shortage is pushing component costs up across the phone industry, squeezing exactly the mid-range tier where Lava lives. It should be a threat. Sunil Raina, Lava's managing director, has decided to treat it as an opening.His logic on the shortage is contrarian but clean. "The issue today is supply chain—component availability. Price is not the issue, because everybody is increasing prices. It's at parity," he says. A phone that sold at 10,000 rupees lands at 15,000; every brand moves together; the market shrinks a little. What was a 15,000-rupee spec sheet earlier now sits at 25,000 or 30,000. Nobody is spared, so nobody is undercut.That parity is where Raina sees room. "In our case, because our market share is not very high, the macro doesn't matter much," he says. "You're taking share from somebody." A brand sitting at two to three percent doesn't need the whole market to grow—it needs to pull buyers off someone bigger. Fifty percent growth is a mountain for the market and a molehill for Lava. Seventeen years in the business, he argues, has bought the supply-chain relationships to actually secure parts when they're scarce, and supply—not demand—is the only real constraint."If we get the supply, we'll do 50 percent or more. The first three months are already 40, 50 percent over last year."That discipline extends to what Lava won't do: chase the price umbrella upward faster than it has to. "I can't expect people to pay 250 just because I'm a new brand," Raina says of how he approaches any market—hold the spec-for-spec value even when costs are climbing, because the moment you break parity is the moment a rival takes the buyer back.It's a coherent bet, and an exposed one. The smallness that has always capped Lava is now the thing it's counting on—a low base that makes steep growth easier to claim, and a shortage that flattens the field just enough for a minor player to move. Whether that's strategy or the only move available is, as ever with Lava, a question of who you ask.
Article Summary: A global memory shortage is pushing component costs up across the phone industry. Sunil Raina, Lava's managing director, has decided to treat it as an opening. A phone that sold at 10,000 rupees lands at 15,000; every brand moves together.

Article Title: It's no country for freshers as IT firms revisit campus hiring playbook
Article Text:
Representative image

Bengaluru:It's no country for freshers.Campus offers are being delayed, onboarding schedules are being pushed back, and in some cases, roles are quietly withdrawn. Joining dates are increasingly tied to shifting business demand, leaving thousands of engineering graduates waiting months—sometimes over a year—for jobs they once assumed were guaranteed. For decades, a campus offer from an IT company came with an unwritten promise: graduate, join training and begin work. That certainty is beginning to disappear.The language companies are using reflects this new reality.IBM recently wrote to candidates waiting for an offer: "While we are trying to get you an early joining, there needs to be appropriate available demand to map you to that project. Once we have an update on the tentative date, our team will get in touch with you and initiate the offer process."At Accenture, after candidates applied for its Custom Software Engineer role, the company informed them: "Due to certain recent developments, we are no longer recruiting for this role." It added that candidates would be contacted if suitable openings matching their profile became available.At Oracle Financial Services Software (OFSS), candidates say offer letters have been delayed.At Cognizant, some GenC recruits have received repeated emails stating their joining date would depend on business requirements, successful background verification and delays linked to the EPFO's UAN portal.One email stated: "Please note that the EPFO portal is currently under maintenance and is expected to be resolved today. Based on the completion of your BGV formalities, the concerned team is working on revising your date of joining. We will keep you informed of any further updates."Another read: "Due to downtime on the UAN website, there may be a delay in processing. Your Date of Joining (DOJ) will be finalized based on business requirements and is subject to the successful completion of the Background Verification (BGV) process. We will keep you informed of further updates. Thank you for your patience and understanding."While administrative issues have contributed to some delays, the repeated references to "business requirements" illustrate how onboarding schedules have become increasingly fluid.The uncertainty has prompted freshers to create their own tracking tools. A 2025 Wipro recruit, frustrated by months of silence over onboarding, launched OnboardWatch, a crowdsourced website where candidates anonymously report whether their offers have been delayed, honoured or revoked.The anxiety comes amid a broader reset in the IT industry's hiring model.India's five largest IT services companies—TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCLTech and Tech Mahindra—collectively reduced their workforce by 6,981 employees in FY26, reversing net additions of 12,718 in FY25. TCS accounted for the biggest reduction, trimming 23,460 employees, while Tech Mahindra cut 1,108.Even as hiring slows at large IT firms, the nature of demand is changing. Nasscom estimates the technology industry's workforce grew by 1.35 lakh to 5.9 million in 2026, only marginally higher than the previous year's addition. Much of the incremental hiring is now coming from global capability centres (GCCs).Companies also say freshers require longer training before they become billable as enterprise technology becomes more complex.Namratha Dharshan, chief business leader at ISG, said opportunities for freshers remain, but employer expectations have changed. "Employers are increasingly prioritising specialised and deep-tech skills, with growing demand for talent that can work effectively with AI and emerging technologies rather than broad-based, generalist capabilities," she said.Phil Fersht, CEO of HFS Research, said the slowdown in campus onboarding reflects a structural shift in the industry's talent model. "Over the next three to five years, the traditional pyramid hiring model that powered India's IT services industry for decades will flatten considerably. Companies will recruit fewer people for generic delivery roles, but demand will increase for graduates with AI literacy, business domain knowledge, data engineering, cybersecurity and industry-specific expertise," he said.Even companies that continue to hire insist recruitment remains demand-led. TCS, which added more than 9,000 employees in the June quarter, says it continues to hire both experienced professionals and fresh graduates.Emails sent to IBM, Accenture, Oracle and Wipro seeking comment did not elicit a response till press time. Cognizant, however, said it remains committed to honouring all offers.Getting the offer, it seems, is no longer the same as getting the job.
Article Summary: Joining dates are increasingly tied to shifting business demand. The uncertainty has prompted freshers to create their own tracking tools. India's five largest IT services companies collectively reduced their workforce by 6,981 employees in FY26, reversing net additions of 12,718 in FY25.

Article Title: Iran war risk: JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon warns of oil shocks, sticky inflation and higher interest rates
Article Text: JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon has warned that the ongoing war in Iran could trigger oil and commodity price shocks, keeping inflation elevated and pushing interest rates higher than current market expectations, Reuters reported.The warning came in his annual letter to shareholders, a day after US President Donald Trump escalated pressure on Iran by threatening to target key infrastructure if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened."Now, because of the war in Iran, we additionally face the potential for significant ongoing oil and commodity price shocks, along with the reshaping of global supply chains, which may lead to stickier inflation and ultimately higher interest rates than markets currently expect," Dimon said, news agency Reuters quoted as saying.Dimon, who has led JPMorgan for two decades, also highlighted broader geopolitical risks, including the war in Ukraine, tensions in the Middle East and friction with China."The challenges we all face are significant," he added.He said it remains uncertain whether the Iran war will achieve US objectives, while warning that nuclear proliferation remains the biggest risk linked to Iran.Markets have already begun pricing in these risks, with expectations of interest rate cuts this year largely fading amid rising inflation concerns triggered by the conflict.Last week, the benchmark S&P 500 index recorded its worst quarterly performance since 2022, weighed down by rising energy prices and geopolitical uncertainty since late February.Dimon noted that the US economy remains resilient, with consumers continuing to earn and spend, and businesses staying broadly healthy, though signs of weakening have emerged.He cautioned that economic strength has been supported by significant government deficit spending and past stimulus, while infrastructure investment needs continue to grow.At the same time, he pointed to positives such as fiscal stimulus under President Trump's "Big, Beautiful Bill", deregulation policies and rising capital expenditure driven by artificial intelligence.On financial stability, Dimon said the $1.8-trillion private credit market "probably" does not pose a systemic risk, despite investor concerns and recent withdrawals from such funds.However, he warned that in a downturn, losses across leveraged lending could exceed expectations as credit standards have weakened.Private credit markets also lack transparency and rigorous valuation benchmarks, increasing the risk of investor exits if conditions worsen, he said.Separately, Dimon criticised revised US capital rules, including Basel III and GSIB surcharge norms, calling aspects of the proposals "nonsensical" and "very flawed".He said JPMorgan’s GSIB surcharge would fall only to 5.0%, a level he described as “absurd” and “un-American”, arguing it penalises the bank’s scale and performance.
Article Summary: JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon warns Iran war could trigger oil and commodity price shocks. He also highlighted broader geopolitical risks, including the war in Ukraine, tensions in the Middle East and friction with China. Dimon criticised revised US capital rules, including Basel III and GSIB surcharge norms, calling aspects of the proposals "nonsensical"

Article Title: How selling Alaska in 1867 was a costly mistake for Russia
Article Text:
Image: Wikipedia

Why Russia agreed to the Alaska purchase

The hidden wealth Russia gave away

Strategic and geopolitical consequences







In 1867, Russia’s decision to sell Alaska to America turned out to be a historic mistake that was to go unnoticed for years to come. The sum paid by the US was only $7,200,000. Although, in the light of the circumstances at the time, it appeared logical, retrospectively, the act looks extremely shortsighted. Russia found itself economically constrained, geographically far away from the region and fearful of its falling into the hands of the British without any compensation. In reality, however, the region had proved immensely valuable both strategically and naturally. Its vast reserves of oil, gold, and natural resources would later transform it into one of the most economically significant territories in the United States.The choice of selling Alaska was based on economic and political reasons. By the middle of the 19th century, the Russian Empire experienced financial difficulties as a result of the expensive Crimean War. It had become increasingly hard for the country to manage such remote and thinly populated areas as Alaska.According to EBSCO , “the Russian-American Company was in decline, and the colony failed to yield any profit”.In addition to this, the lucrative fur trade had declined, making the territory much less economically valuable for the empire.At the same time, Russia was afraid that Britain might capture Alaska in case another war broke out between the two countries. Selling the colony to the friendly United States appeared as a logical step. As the Office of the Historian states, William H. Seward, U.S. Secretary of State, “it was a chance to expand its influence in North America and hinder further growth of Britain”.For Russia, the transaction was a chance to minimise the losses. However, it greatly undervalued the potential of Alaska.Another thing that Russia did not expect was the amount of riches that Alaska possessed. Within several decades after the acquisition, the state saw discoveries of enormous deposits of gold, oil, and other minerals.“Alaska has produced more than 40 million ounces of gold,” according to the US Geological Survey. Furthermore, Alaska is home to some of the world's most significant undeveloped mineral deposits, according to the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) . But even more importantly, the state's oil deposits turned out to be extremely valuable. The Prudhoe Bay oil field was discovered in 1968 and became one of North America's biggest.In retrospect, the amount paid by the Americans, $7.2 million, or two cents per acre, was rather laughable. According to Howard I. Kushner , the deal “robbed Russia of an enormously rich territory, the true value of which would only be recognised in the twentieth century.”In addition to resources, there is the question of strategy and how important Alaska is to the United States from a strategic standpoint. This is a very good place when it comes to defence strategy. It borders on the Arctic Ocean and also happens to be close to Russia.During the period of the Cold War, the Alaskan region became a frontier area for the United States, becoming home to military facilities and warning systems.According to Col. Michael J. Forsyth, U.S. Army, the closeness of Russia and Alaska, only about ninety kilometres apart across the Bering Strait, meant that this region became highly significant to the United States' defence plans.From today's point of view, the strategic location of Alaska makes the state very important to the policies of the U.S. related to the Arctic, energy security, and even environmental policies.Thinking back, what seems clear to us now is that the Russians sold Alaska due to immediate needs rather than future considerations. In order to solve the problem, they lost a valuable resource for Russia.Conclusively, the Alaska Purchase should not have been done as it has had a significant impact that will remain throughout history.
Article Summary: Russia sold Alaska to the U.S. in 1867 for only $7,200,000. The sale was based on economic and political reasons. However, the region proved immensely valuable both strategically and naturally. Alaska is home to some of the world's most significant undeveloped mineral deposits. The Prudhoe Bay oil field was discovered in 1968.

Article Title: AI data centers are causing 'stress' not just to tech companies, but also private insurers
Article Text:
Massive investments, growing risks



Unique challenges for data centre insurance companies





AI data centers are reportedly putting new pressure on the insurance industry. According to a CNBC report, experts warn that the rapid growth of these projects is becoming a “stress test” not just for tech companies, but also for private insurers and lenders. Consulting firm McKinsey estimates that global spending on data centers could reach $7 trillion by 2030. As costs rise, tech giants are increasingly relying on private equity, private credit and debt to fund these projects. The scale of investments is creating challenges for insurers.“When you put $10 to $20 billion plus in a single location, it creates capacity issues in the marketplace,” said Tom Harper, data center leader at insurance broker Gallagher as quoted in the CNBC report.He added that the sector has been a “real stress test” over the last few years, as insurers struggle to provide coverage for such large and complex projects. While these facilities are seen as high-quality assets, Harper said insuring them has become difficult due to their size and risk concentration.Private data center deals have crossed $10 billion regularly, with some reaching as high as $40 billion. Companies including Nvidia, Microsoft and BlackRock have already taken part in large investment deals.Rajat Rana, partner at Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan, described the scale as unprecedented. “We’re talking about trillions of dollars, and almost going back to the same cycle where there’s almost no transparency about the financing structures — the scale is astronomical,” he said. He added that the boom in AI data centers could be “the largest peacetime investment project in human history.”Experts say the way these projects are financed is adding another layer of risk. Much of the funding is happening through complex and less transparent debt structures. Rana warned that this trend feels like “deja vu,” referring to past financial cycles. He said the lack of transparency could create risks for investors, insurers and pension funds.As stated in the CNBC report, data centers offer unique challenges due to their mix of real estate and advanced technology. Insurers are now designing custom policies to manage these risks. However, challenges remain. Large facilities are often located in areas prone to extreme weather, and they store expensive equipment, sometimes even before installation.Another key concern is the mismatch between the lifespan of data centers and the hardware they use. While data centers can last decades, GPUs — the chips powering AI — typically last around seven years. Rana described this as a “GPU debt treadmill,” saying, “This is almost like a treadmill that these AI data centers are running on.”He explained that companies may need to keep raising debt to upgrade infrastructure as technology evolves.
Article Summary: Private data center deals have crossed $10 billion regularly, with some reaching as high as $40 billion. McKinsey estimates that global spending on data centers could reach $7 trillion by 2030. Tech giants are increasingly relying on private equity, private credit and debt to fund these projects.

Article Title: Mumbai University slashes intake, fines 102 law and BEd colleges for lacking approved teachers
Article Text:
Mumbai:

After initially debarring several law and BEd colleges from this year's admission process, Mumbai University has allowed them to participate in it, but with hefty fines and a substantial cut in student intake. In some cases, the university issued affiliation certificates, but they have now been cancelled.The university reduced the intake capacity by 50% for 39 law and 29 BEd colleges that do not have an approved principal or a single approved teacher on their rolls. Additionally, it has imposed a fine of Rs 10 lakh on each. In another category of colleges, which have less than 50% approved teachers, the university has cut the student intake by 25% and has asked them to pay a fine of Rs 5 lakh each. In the second category, there are 11 law colleges and 23 BEd colleges. Some of these colleges plan to move court against the university's decision.This is the first time the university has taken such stringent action against affiliated colleges for failing to appoint approved teachers. The collective reduction in intake is expected to affect around 6,000 to 8,000 seats during the ongoing admission season.In a letter sent to a law college on Monday, the university stated that the affiliation certificate issued for the 2026-27 academic year stands null and void.It said the certificate had been issued due to a technical error by the affiliation department and added that any students admitted beyond the revised 75% intake capacity will not be enrolled by the university. The college will be solely responsible for such admissions, it stated.The university's registrar, Prasad Karande, told TOI that the action taken is only on the basis of lack of approved teachers and that the university has not yet considered infrastructure. A principal said that no local inquiry committee visited their college before issuing such a diktat and therefore, colleges are planning to move court. A university official, though, said these colleges had given an undertaking last year too and did not fulfil the requirements.There are 110 law colleges and 80 BEd colleges affiliated to Mumbai University.
Article Summary: Mumbai University initially debarred several law and BEd colleges from this year's admission process. The university has allowed them to participate, but with hefty fines and a substantial cut in student intake. In some cases, the university issued affiliation certificates, but they have now been cancelled. Some of these colleges plan to move court against the university's decision.

Article Title: Maharashtra selects Ahmedabad organisation for residential units in 6 hospitals for patients’ kin
Article Text: Mumbai: The Maharashtra govt has selected Ahmedabad-based Sevadan Arogya Foundation to build, operate and maintain residential facilities for relatives of patients at six govt medical colleges and affiliated hospitals under a ‘no-profit, no-loss’ model.A Government Resolution (GR) issued on Monday said facilities will come up at govt medical institutions in Mumbai, Pune, Nagpur, Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar, Kolhapur and Yavatmal.“The state will execute an MoU with the Foundation for 49 years. Land required for the project will be leased to the organisation at a nominal annual rent of Re 1, and the lease may be renewed once for a further period of up to 49 years in accordance with the prevailing govt policy,” it said.However, the ownership, possession and control of the land will remain with the Maharashtra govt, the GR added.
Article Summary: The Maharashtra govt has selected Ahmedabad-based Sevadan Arogya Foundation to build, operate and maintain residential facilities for relatives of patients. The facilities will come up at govt medical institutions in Mumbai, Pune, Nagpur, Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar, Kolhapur and Yavatmal.

Article Title: Arts enrolment in MU’s aided colleges drops 55% in 15 yrs; experts cite failure to evolve
Article Text:
Fewer young people are now choosing to study humanities or commerce

Mumbai:

The classrooms are still there, as are the old college buildings that have opened their doors to generations of Mumbai students. But in the aided colleges affiliated with Mumbai University (MU), fewer young people are now choosing to study humanities or commerce.Data from the university shows that first-year enrolment in arts courses has fallen by a staggering 54% over the past 15 years—the sharpest decline across traditional undergraduate programmes. Commerce admissions have dropped by nearly 20% over the same period. Science, in contrast, has managed to record an improvement in student numbers, going up by nearly 11% in the same period.Educationists see the figures as more than a change in academic preference. They describe them as signs of the gradual erosion of public institutions that have historically made higher education accessible to students across social and economic backgrounds.They blame the steady slide on a combination of declining academic standards, outdated syllabi and the growing appeal of specialised, industry-oriented programmes that appear to promise better employment prospects. Unless aided colleges update their courses, invest in teaching and respond to a rapidly changing job market, experts fear that conventional degree programmes will continue to lose students.The consequences, they warn, will be felt most sharply by those who cannot afford expensive private colleges or specialised professional courses.“Our society has become so technical that it no longer recognises the importance of the humanities,” says former St Xavier’s College principal Fr Frazer Mascarenhas. “History, sociology, psychology and English literature develop perspectives and social skills that technical education cannot provide. We are progressing towards becoming a technocratic society, but one that does not necessarily feel fulfilled. This is a serious decline in human civilisation.”A former MU vice-chancellor says the decline in aided programmes was largely the result of their failure to change with the times. “The respective boards of studies have not thought seriously about making courses relevant and syllabi have not been updated with contemporary content,” he says. He points out that while universities elsewhere had reinvented the humanities through liberal arts programmes, aided colleges continued to offer the same old combination of subjects. “Liberal arts in private universities of India and abroad allow students to choose from a diverse basket that may include law, management, economics and the humanities. Here, the combination of subjects has remained largely unchanged, and humanities courses continue to be taught in their traditional form. They must be modified to meet the needs of today’s students,” he says.He adds that colleges are also struggling because teaching posts remain vacant, while the procedure for appointing the faculty continues to be archaic and cumbersome. “Courses cannot improve unless colleges are able to recruit good teachers and regularly revise what they teach,” he says.S S Mantha, former AICTE chairperson, says students seek degrees that have a clear connection with employment and are, therefore, moving towards AI, data science, cybersecurity, business analytics and digital marketing, among others. “Over the past decade, there has been a rapid increase in private universities, skill universities, deemed universities, autonomous colleges, foreign university campuses and online and hybrid education providers. Many institutions now offer interdisciplinary and industry-oriented programmes, increasing competition for students who would have previously enrolled in traditional degrees. These universities spread the same pool of applicants across many more institutions.”
Article Summary: Mumbai University data shows that first-year enrolment in arts courses has fallen by a staggering 54% over the past 15 years. Commerce admissions have dropped by nearly 20%. Science, in contrast, has managed to record an improvement in student numbers, going up by nearly 11%.

Article Title: Around 1kg fake silver jewellery found in donation box at Purushottam temple
Article Text:
Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar:

Nearly one kilogram of imitation silver jewellery was found in the donation box of the renowned Purushottam temple at Purushottampuri in Beed district after jewellers examined offerings made by the devotees during the recently concluded Adhik Maas pilgrimage.Traditionally, devotees donate precious metals at this temple during the Adhik Maas period. The scrutiny of jewellery and ornaments donated at the temple revealed that while devotees offered around 9 grams of gold and 446 grams of genuine silver jewellery, nearly 973 grams of silver-coloured imitation ornaments had also been placed in the donation box.Temple committee chairperson Vijay Golekar said the month-long pilgrimage concluded peacefully with the cooperation of the temple administration, villagers, public representatives, police, health and revenue department officials.“Devotees donated around Rs 1.5 crore to Lord Purushottam. They also offered gold and silver ornaments. However, nearly 973 grams of the silver jewellery was found to be fake during examination," he said.Those associated with the temple administration suspect that the possibility of jewellers selling silver colour polished metals by falsely claiming it to be original silver to the gullible devotees, who ended up donating it to the temple.The pilgrimage, held from May 17 to June 15 during the Adhik Maas period, witnessed an unprecedented turnout with an estimated 50 lakh devotees visiting the temple situated on the banks of the Godavari river in Majalgaon taluka.The temple is considered the country's only shrine dedicated to Lord Purushottam and is associated with the sacred Chakratirth, making a holy dip in the Godavari and darshan of the deity especially significant during Adhik Maas.According to the temple committee, devotees contributed around Rs 1.5 crore in cash during the pilgrimage, in addition to donations of gold and silver ornaments. The committee also noted a substantial rise in footfall compared to the previous Adhik Maas pilgrimage held three years ago.Temple authorities said the discovery of imitation silver ornaments came to light only after the donated jewellery was examined by authorised jewellers following the conclusion of the pilgrimage. No complaint has been registered, and the committee has not indicated whether any further action will be initiated regarding the fake jewellery donations.
Article Summary: Traditionally, devotees donate precious metals at this temple during the Adhik Maas period. The scrutiny of jewellery and ornaments donated at the temple revealed that while devotees offered around 9 grams of gold and 446 grams of genuine silver jewellery.

Article Title: Aurangabad bench of Bombay high court issues notices on plea seeking CBI, ED probe into alleged Rs 3,000 crore investment scam
Article Text:
Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar:

The Aurangabad bench of the Bombay high court on Monday issued notices on a criminal writ petition seeking transfer of the investigation into the alleged Tredz-Infinite Becon-Sispay investment scam — worth over Rs 3,000 crore — to the Central Bureau of Investigation and the Enforcement Directorate (ED).A division bench of Justices Sandipkumar More and Vaishali Patil-Jadhav sought responses from all respondents, including the Union govt, after hearing the petition.The petition has been filed by Oneness for Solution Foundation, a registered society claiming to represent around 3,000 alleged victims of the scam, along with Nitin Gangarde, the original complainant in an FIR registered at the Shrigonda police station in Ahilyanagar district.Appearing for the petitioners, advocates Ashish Sonawane and Vilas Sonawane told the court that at least eight FIRs have been registered at different police stations across Maharashtra in connection with the alleged fraud and that nearly 1.3 lakh investors are believed to have been duped.According to the petition, the accused masterminds of the massive Ponzi scam allegedly operated a vast network comprising partnership firms, LLPs, private limited companies and a multi-state credit cooperative society, promising monthly returns of 8-10% through investments in the capital market.The plea further alleges that investors were lured by using the name of Tredz Investment Private Limited, a SEBI-, NSE- and BSE-registered entity. It refers to a Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) adjudication order under which the regulator allegedly cancelled the company’s registration and imposed a penalty of Rs 1 crore after recording the alleged modus operandi.The petitioners have also questioned the manner of the ongoing investigation. They contended that while provisions of the Banning of Unregulated Deposit Schemes (BUDS) Act have been invoked in cases registered in Pune and Ahilyanagar, the investigation has not been transferred to the CBI as envisaged under the law. The plea further alleges that partial chargesheets filed in some cases indicate serious lapses in the probe.The petition additionally claims that the proceeds of the alleged fraud were laundered through banking channels and that some accused have acquired foreign citizenship or overseas residency while allegedly transferring funds abroad through offshore entities and cryptocurrencies.Seeking intervention by central agencies, the petition has urged the HC to direct an ED investigation under the Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA), initiate action against fugitive accused under relevant laws, and hand over the overall investigation to the CBI.
Article Summary: The petition has been filed by Oneness for Solution Foundation, a registered society claiming to represent around 3,000 alleged victims of the scam. A division bench of Justices Sandipkumar More and Vaishali Patil-Jadhav sought responses from all respondents, including the Union govt, after hearing the petition.

Article Title: HC refuses to revive delayed divorce appeal after ex-husband’s remarriage
Article Text:
Bombay high court’s Aurangabad bench

Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar

:

Bombay HC’s Aurangabad bench has held that once the statutory period for challenging a divorce decree expires and a former spouse legally remarries, a delayed appeal against the divorce becomes infructuous and condoning the delay serves no useful purpose.A division bench of Justice Sandipkumar More and Justice Vaishali Patil-Jadhav dismissed a woman’s plea seeking condonation of a 381-day delay in filing an appeal against a family court decree dissolving her marriage. The court also rejected the proposed appeal, holding that it had become infructuous from the outset as the woman’s former husband had contracted a valid second marriage after the limitation period for filing an appeal had lapsed.The bench observed that under Section 15 of the Hindu Marriage Act and Section 19 of the Family Courts Act, either spouse was entitled to remarry if no appeal was filed within the prescribed limitation period. Once that period expires, the divorce decree attains finality, enabling the parties to lawfully enter into another marriage, it observed.The woman had contended that she became aware of the divorce decree only during proceedings under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act.She argued that her former husband’s subsequent remarriage could not extinguish her statutory right to appeal and sought support from principles applied in service law and the doctrine of lis pendens.Rejecting the argument, the court held that principles governing service matters and property disputes were not applicable to matrimonial litigation. “The reliance placed on principles from Service Law or the doctrine of lis pendens under Section 52 of the Transfer of Property Act are inapplicable to the facts of the present case,” the bench observed.Relying on the Supreme Court’s decision in Krishnaveni Rai vs Pankaj Rai and an earlier Bombay HC judgment, the bench held that a second marriage solemnised after the expiry of the appeal period remained valid and could not be invalidated merely because a delayed appeal was filed later. While the right to appeal was a substantive right, it remained subject to the law of limitation, the bench said.The court also found the woman’s explanation for the delay unconvincing, noting that she had participated in the divorce proceedings by filing a written statement but subsequently chose to remain absent. It further clarified that dismissal of the appeal would not bar the woman from independently pursuing a claim for permanent alimony before the appropriate forum.
Article Summary: Bombay HC’s Aurangabad bench has held that once the statutory period for challenging a divorce decree expires and a former spouse legally remarries, a delayed appeal against the divorce becomes infructuous. The court also rejected the proposed appeal, holding that it had become inf Structuous from the outset.
